speaker: interviewer
text: This is Adela Manheimer, and her daughter and granddaughter. And I would like, Adela, your daughter, to say whatever she thinks about the interview and what she feels. Thank you very much. Is that all right? This is the last tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Rosa Eichenbaum. This is my mother, Adela Manheimer. She's a concentration camp survivor. She has taught me how to live and how to love. She has been the best mother any mother could be. I'm sorry for all that she has had to endure in her life. But she has given me many important lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm also grateful to the Shoah and Masha Loen, who has offered to record my mother's history so that I can pass it on to my children, and they can pass it on to their children. This is my daughter, Ariella Eichenbaum, who's 15, and very talented and beautiful, who has also learned a lot from my mother. Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to say that I love my grandmother and my family very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Adela Manheimer in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: My house. OK, This-- the first picture with the tie was taken in Germany in, like, '46. And the second in '47. And the third was already in, in Munich, also in Germany. I was in Germany in '40-- '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was all of you, wasn't it, Adela.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is just the head. Can you make higher, please? OK, and this is Adela Manheimer taken in 1990 in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, and this is mine family at the bar mitzvah of my son. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year, Adela, was that taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us who's in the picture from your left--

speaker: interviewee
text: On this picture is me, Adela Manheimer, Rosa Lee, and Aron, and Wolf.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter, Rosa Lee in, in 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, wait a moment. When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was maybe three years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that your granddaughter or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son. And the photograph is-- was taken in Germany, Feldafing. And he was in 1951-- no. Not [INAUDIBLE]. He was born in '48 when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good looking guy. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Rosa Lee, with her husband in 1993.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Aron Manheimer and Judy Hirt Manheimer in 1992.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Connecticut. In Ridgefield. Ridgefield, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, what I want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my six grandchildren, Noah, Ariella, Isaac, Jeremy, Talia, and Mimi-- Miriam. And this is taken in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Los Angeles in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adela, I would like to go over the picture again, very slowly. From the oldest grandson to the littlest one, very slowly--

speaker: interviewer
text: --their name, and their second name, OK? Because some of them have a different name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Noah Hirt-Manheimer. Left. From left, is Noah Hirt-Manheimer, Ariella Eichenbaum, Isaac Hirt-Manheimer, Jeremy David Eichenbaum, Talia Eichenbaum, and Miriam Hirt-Manheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Adela, you told me that your son is very interested in the survivors. He writes about them. This is a magazine he edited. Would you tell us a little bit about it? He's the editor of what? It's called what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the editor of the magazine in reform Judaism. And his wife Judy is also the copy editor in this magazine. He is speaking to a lot of synagogues and also teaching in Westport Temple, which is the largest temple, Connecticut, and the Holocaust for teenage children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the magazine that is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the magazine what he edited for a long time ago. But it's very, very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he's the second generation. He speaks a lot in temples about the, the Crystal Night and all about-- all over as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the friend of Elie Wiesel. He published his book, and also Samuel Pisar, which is the international lawyer, and is a survivor from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And I'm very impressed. And now that we're through with that, we are going to take a picture of you and Solomon. And with that we'll end our session.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a picture of me, Adela Manheimer, and Solomon Urman taken, like, '87, 1987. And it's in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you make a beautiful couple. And with that, with that, we'll end our interview with Adela and Solomon. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for all your hard work. And we appreciate very much. You are very nice. And we are-- the whole day is very exciting for us, through tears and laughter.
